ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pork belly food product entry; no exact entry for braised pork belly with soft-boiled egg as a composite dish. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked pig belly; no composite dish code for braised pork belly with egg exists. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Chinese braised dish. Left null per ontology honesty policy.

serving.description: 1 plate (pork belly with sauce and 1 egg)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one plate comprising approximately 200–220g of braised pork belly with sauce and one soft-boiled egg (approximately 50–60g), totalling around 300g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, belly, raw (NDB 10006) and Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (NDB 01129); composite estimate weighted for braised preparation with soy-based sauce
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for braised pork belly with soft-boiled egg as a composite dish. Values are derived by weighting USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pork belly (approximately 70% by weight) and hard-boiled egg (approximately 20% by weight) with an estimated soy-based braising sauce contribution (approximately 10% by weight). The input record values (energy 216.67 kcal, protein 11.67g, fat 16.67g, carbohydrate 5.0g, sodium 600mg) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a direct entry for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-egg dish with soy-braised sauce; GI anchored to pork-dominant protein-fat dish with low carbohydrate content.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork belly with soft-boiled egg. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed pork dishes with soy sauce and low net carbohydrate content (~5g/100g). The dish is protein- and fat-dominant, which substantially blunts postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 300g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.0) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.8 accounting for sauce sugar contribution. This is a conservative analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 600mg/100g (1800mg per 300g serving) substantially exceeds the recommended 600mg per meal limit for hypertensive individuals; saturated fat at 6.67g/100g further contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid consuming the braising sauce, request reduced soy sauce in preparation, and limit portion size to no more than 100–150g to keep sodium intake below 900mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is low at 5.0g/100g and GI is estimated low (~35), but high saturated fat (6.67g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption, and rock sugar in the braising sauce adds refined carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–150g) occasionally, but should request reduced rock sugar in the braising sauce, avoid the sauce itself to limit refined sugar and sodium, and monitor postprandial glucose given the saturated fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.67g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g threshold for cardiovascular concern, cholesterol is 83.33mg/100g (250mg per 300g serving), and sodium at 600mg/100g compounds cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit braised pork belly to occasional small portions (under 100g), avoid the braising sauce to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and consider substituting with leaner pork cuts or skinless poultry to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (6.67g/100g) and red meat arachidonic acid content promote pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); sodium at 600mg/100g worsens joint tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid braised pork belly due to its high saturated fat and red meat content, avoid the braising sauce to reduce sodium-driven joint swelling, and note that ginger and cinnamon in the recipe provide insufficient anti-inflammatory offset.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pork Belly with Soft-Boiled Egg (红烧肉配溏心蛋, Hóng shāo ròu pèi táng xīn dàn) is a classic Chinese comfort dish widely enjoyed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The dish features thick slices of pork belly — comprising alternating layers of fat and lean meat — slow-braised for one to two hours in a rich, savoury-sweet sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, rock sugar, ginger, garlic, star anise, and cinnamon. The prolonged braising renders the fat layers gelatinous and the lean meat tender, producing a deeply flavoured, glossy, mahogany-coloured dish. It is served alongside one or more soft-boiled eggs (溏心蛋) that have been marinated in the braising liquid, giving them a soy-stained exterior and a jammy, semi-liquid yolk. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 217 kcal, 11.7g protein, 16.7g fat (of which 6.7g is saturated), 5.0g carbohydrate, and 600mg sodium. The high sodium content (driven by soy sauce) and saturated fat content make this dish unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycemic index (~35) are moderately favourable, but the saturated fat load warrants portion control. A standard serving is one plate of approximately 300g. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains soy, egg, and gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central) carries an exact entry for braised pork belly with soft-boiled egg as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pork belly and hard-boiled egg, with soy-based sauce contribution estimated. The input record values are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite braised preparation are not available in a single approved source entry as of April 2026. 2. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible): braised pork belly with egg contains no meaningful vitamin C source; heat from prolonged braising destroys any residual vitamin C from aromatics. Confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central cooked pork and egg entries. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.17g/100g: small naturally occurring trans fat from ruminant-adjacent pork fat; retained from input record, consistent with USDA data for cooked pork belly. 4. The input record description noted a GrabFood listing inaccuracy (described as 'Roasted Duck dish'); this has been corrected — the record correctly identifies the dish as Braised Pork Belly with Soft-Boiled Egg. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (~35, low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database framework for protein-fat-dominant dishes with low net carbohydrate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the published literature. 6. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (35 × 15.0g carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.25, conservatively adjusted to 5.8 to account for rock sugar in braising sauce. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are closest_parent matches; no exact composite dish code exists for this preparation.
